items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.q: Which colours typically advance in a composition?
items.why: Students conflate darkness with advancement. In atmospheric perspective, COOLER colours recede (think distant blue mountains) and warmer colours advance, regardless of darkness.
It's Van Gogh. “Monet” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Linseed oil. “Water” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate century when oil painting became dominant in Europe?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 century. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
